title: Fidelity-Aware Frequency Allocation and Transpilation Co-Design for Tunable Coupler Quantum Systems
abstract: Frequency crowding is a fundamental limitation in superconducting quantum architectures, particularly in tunable-coupler systems. We present a framework that explicitly models both coherent spectator-induced errors and incoherent lifetime effects through an error budgeting approach. Using this model, we analyze how frequency crowding impacts gate fidelity as module size and connectivity scale, and formulate a constrained optimization problem to assign qubit and coupler frequencies under realistic separation and hardware constraints. We demonstrate scalable frequency allocation strategies that minimize spectator-induced errors. We further show that increasing qubit count and coupling density within a module leads to a fidelity-connectivity tradeoff. To explore the benefits at the system scale, we have developed a noise-aware transpilation approach called FINESSE, which minimizes error by selecting high-fidelity paths that satisfy connectivity via SWAP insertion while jointly optimizing downstream gate execution. We demonstrate this physics-informed architecture-transpilation co-design approach for a SNAIL-based third-order coupler that natively realizes the $\sqrt{iSWAP}$ basis with frequency aware gate fidelities. On SNAIL architectures, FINESSE achieves an average 8.9% reduction in log-infidelity cost and 6.8% reduction in circuit depth vs. SABRE. We also compare results on IBM Brisbane's architecture.
comment: 14 pages, 16 figures, prepared initially for ASPLOS

bite.hook: A new quantum co-design framework slashes error rates by 8.9% and circuit depth by 6.8% on SNAIL architectures.
tldr: They modeled frequency crowding errors in tunable-coupler quantum systems to optimize qubit and coupler frequency assignments.
They developed FINESSE, a noise-aware transpilation approach that selects high-fidelity paths and optimizes gate execution.
They achieved an 8.9% reduction in log-infidelity cost and a 6.8% reduction in circuit depth compared to the standard SABRE compiler.
bite.whyItMatters: As quantum computers scale up, packing more qubits together causes them to interfere with each other. This co-design approach helps squeeze higher performance out of existing hardware without needing to physically rebuild the chips.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to have dozens of private conversations in a small, crowded room. If everyone speaks at a similar pitch, it becomes a noisy mess; this is called frequency crowding. This new system acts like a smart seating planner and translator, assigning unique voice pitches to speakers and mapping out the quietest paths for messages to travel.

keyNumbers.label: reduction in log-infidelity cost
keyNumbers.context: Achieved by the FINESSE framework on SNAIL-based architectures compared to the SABRE compiler.

keyNumbers.label: reduction in circuit depth
keyNumbers.context: Achieved by FINESSE, meaning quantum programs run faster and experience less environmental decay.

glossary.shortDef: Overlapping operating frequencies in quantum processors that cause unwanted crosstalk between qubits.
glossary.eli5Def: When qubits are tuned too close to the same 'radio station', making them accidentally interfere with each other.

glossary.shortDef: The process of translating an abstract quantum circuit into physical instructions optimized for specific hardware.
glossary.eli5Def: Translating a general recipe so it works perfectly with the exact pots, pans, and stove temperature of a specific kitchen.

glossary.shortDef: Superconducting Nonlinear Asymmetric Inductive Element, a type of dipole element used for quantum coupling.
glossary.eli5Def: A specialized superconducting loop that acts like a highly adjustable traffic controller for quantum information.
